Barrier: Lack of interest on the part of building decision-makers in financial instruments
Resulting From: Grant culture with 100% grants in prior renovation programs discourages use of financial instruments
Lack of multi-year budgeting, restrictions of retention of cost savings, limitations on using operating savings for capital expenses
Lengthy approval chains and rigid procurement procedures as well as requirements for lowest up-front cost are unfavorable to energy-efficient renovation projects
Existing regulations limit contract length and therefore the ability of ESCOs to engage in performance contracts with public agencies

Barrier: Limited capacity for project implementation in municipalities and central government agencies
Resulting From: Many municipalities and central government agencies do not have capable staff that have adequate skills and capabilities to prepare and manage building renovation projects.

Barrier: High investment needed to provide adequate comfort levels
Resulting From: Many public buildings are insufficiently heated, and additional investments are needed to bring them to standard comfort levels.
Municipalities have limits on borrowing and are often already at their borrowing limits. If the renovation project requires debt financing many municipalities are unable to acquire the debt

Barrier: Lack of ability to provide collateral
Resulting From: Public agencies generally unable to provide adequate collateral for bank loans
Many municipalities and some central agencies are unable to meet the financial institutions credit criteria

Barrier: Limited interest and capacity of ESCOs to engage with public agencies
Resulting From: Inadequate regulatory framework limits ESCO contracting with public agencies. Also, issues related to risk of non-payment deters ESCOs.

Barrier: Lack of standard tools and templates
Resulting From: There are no standard tools and templates for contracts between public agencies and renovation contractors or ESCOs
